perturbation.original_text: 1.1 “Invention”, the Pipeline Piranha Invention, Patent Pending #103573330A, shall mean all information relating to products, applications, systems, components, technologies and business topics. Including but not limited to prototypes, drawings, data, trade secrets and in tellectual property relating to the “Patent Pending” invention named Pipeline Piranha.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1 “Invention”, the Pipeline Piranha Invention, Patent Pending #103573330A, shall mean all information relating to products, applications, systems, components, technologies and business topics. Including but not limited to prototypes, drawings, data, trade secrets and intellectual property. This definition does not include source code.
perturbation.explanation: By explicitly excluding 'source code' from the definition of 'Invention', the contract creates ambiguity. Source code is often a critical part of an invention and is typically protected as intellectual property.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Trade Secret Definition under the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA)
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA defines trade secrets as information including 'a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process' that derives independent economic value from not being generally known and is subject to reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. Source code often meets this definition. Excluding it contradicts the DTSA's broad protection of trade secrets.

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the DTSA by explicitly excluding 'source code' from the definition of 'Invention.' Source code is often a critical part of an invention and is typically protected as intellectual property. The Law Explanation supports the idea that 'source code often meets this definition [of trade secrets].' While scraped snippet 2 is empty, scraped snippet 1 from 18 USC 1839 gives the definition of trade secrets, and the definition given in the Law Explanation is in line with that definition. Excluding source code could undermine trade secret protection for inventions.

perturbation.original_text: 1.2 “Confidential Information” shall mean all information provided by Disclosing Party with respect to the Invention regardless of whether it is written, oral, audio tapes, video tapes, computer disc s, machines, prototypes, designs, specifications, articles of manufacture, drawings, huma n or machine readable documents. Confidential Information shall also include all information related to the Invention provided by Disclosing Pa rty to Receiving Party prior to the signing Confidentiality and Non-Disclosure Agreement Page 2 of this agreement. Confidential Information shall not include any information in the public domain at the time of the disclosure, or subsequently comes within the public domain without fault of the Receiving Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.2 “Confidential Information” shall mean all information provided by Disclosing Party with respect to the Invention regardless of whether it is written, oral, audio tapes, video tapes, computer disc s, machines, prototypes, designs, specifications, articles of manufacture, drawings, huma n or machine readable documents. Confidential Information shall also include all information related to the Invention provided by Disclosing Pa rty to Receiving Party prior to the signing of this agreement. Confidential Information shall not include any information already known to the Receiving Party prior to disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: By stating confidential information does not include information 'already known' to the receiving party, it contradicts the definition of trade secret, which can include information known by a party if kept confidential. This changes the scope of what is legally protected.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA defines a trade secret, in part, as information that 'derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use'. The modified text contradicts this because the fact that the receiving party already knows the information does not automatically disqualify it from being a trade secret if the information derives independent economic value from not being generally known to others.

perturbation.justification: The modification contradicts the standard for trade secret protection by excluding information already known to the receiving party. The Law Explanation states that information can still be a trade secret, even if known by the receiving party, if it has economic value from being not generally known to others. This contradicts the modified text's exclusion of 'information already known to the Receiving Party prior to disclosure' from being considered confidential information. Scraped snippet 2 is not usable, however the LLM explanation and the perturbation explanation both sufficiently and clearly describe this contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3. RETURN OF CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION All information provided by the Disclosing Party shall remain the property of the Disclosing Party. Receiving Party agrees to return all Confidential Information to Disclosing Party within 15 days of written demand by Disclosing Party. When the Receiving Party has finished reviewing the information provided by the Disclosing Party and has made a decision as to whet her or not to work with the Disclosing Party, Receiving Party shall return all information to the Disclosing Party without retaining any copies.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. RETURN OF CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION All information provided by the Disclosing Party shall remain the property of the Disclosing Party. Receiving Party agrees to return all Confidential Information to Disclosing Party within 15 days of written demand by Disclosing Party, unless the Receiving Party can prove independent development of similar information. When the Receiving Party has finished reviewing the information provided by the Disclosing Party and has made a decision as to whet her or not to work with the Disclosing Party, Receiving Party shall return all information to the Disclosing Party without retaining any copies.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'unless the Receiving Party can prove independent development of similar information' creates a loophole that contradicts typical trade secret protection. Trade secret law generally protects against misappropriation, regardless of independent development.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA defines misappropriation as including acquisition of a trade secret by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means. Even if the receiving party independently developed similar information, using the disclosing party's trade secret as a starting point could still constitute misappropriation. The added clause weakens the protection afforded by the UTSA.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces a clause that allows the Receiving Party to avoid returning confidential information if they can prove independent development of similar information. This directly contradicts the principle of trade secret protection, where misappropriation can occur even with independent development. The Law Explanation states, "Even if the receiving party independently developed similar information, using the disclosing party's trade secret as a starting point could still constitute misappropriation. The added clause weakens the protection afforded by the UTSA." While the provided snippets are not directly helpful, the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation sufficiently demonstrate the contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 5. GOVERNING LAW This agreement and all questions relating to its validity, interpretation, performance and enforcement (including, without limitation, provisions concerning limitations of actions), shall be governed by and cons trued in accordance with the laws of the State Confidentiality and Non-Disclosure Agreement Page 4 of Oregon, notwithstanding any conflict-of -laws doctrines of such state or other jurisdiction to the contrary, and without th e aid of any canon, custom or rule of law requiring construction against the draftsman.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. GOVERNING LAW This agreement and all questions relating to its validity, interpretation, performance and enforcement shall be governed by the laws of Oregon, except for matters relating to intellectual property, which shall be governed by the sole discretion of the Receiving Party.
perturbation.explanation: By giving the Receiving Party the sole discretion in determining the governing law for intellectual property disputes, this clause contradicts established federal intellectual property law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Patent Law and Copyright Law
perturbation.law_citation: 35 U.S.C. § 1 et seq. (Patent Law) and 17 U.S.C. § 101 et seq. (Copyright Law)
perturbation.law_explanation: Federal patent law (35 U.S.C. § 1 et seq.) and copyright law (17 U.S.C. § 101 et seq.) are federal statutes that preempt state law in many areas. Giving the Receiving Party sole discretion to choose the governing law undermines this federal preemption and creates uncertainty about which laws will apply to intellectual property disputes.

perturbation.justification: The changed text grants the Receiving Party 'sole discretion' over the governing law for intellectual property matters, directly conflicting with the principle that federal law (patent and copyright) preempts state law in many areas of intellectual property. The scraped snippets are not helpful, but the law explanation indicates that granting the receiving party 'sole discretion' undermines federal preemption and creates uncertainty about applicable laws, establishing a legal contradiction. This alteration empowers a single party to potentially bypass established legal frameworks, representing a significant deviation from standard legal governance.

perturbation.original_text: 10. ARBITRATION Any controversy or claim arising out of or relating to this agreement, or the breach thereof, shall be resolved by arbitration conducted by the Commercial Division of the American Arbitration Asso ciation and in accordance with the rules thereof, or in any other convenient forum agreed to in writing by the parties. Any arbitration award shall be final and binding, and judgment upon the award rendered pursuant to such arbitration may be entered in any court of proper jurisdiction. Notwithstanding the forego ing, either party may seek and obtain temporary injunctive relief from any court of competent jurisdiction against any improper disclosure of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. ARBITRATION Any controversy or claim arising out of or relating to this agreement, or the breach thereof, shall be resolved by arbitration. The arbitrator's decision shall be considered advisory and non-binding, except in cases involving intellectual property, where the Receiving Party's interpretation of the Confidential Information will be final.
perturbation.explanation: Making the arbitration decision advisory and giving the Receiving Party the final say on interpreting confidential information in intellectual property cases directly undermines the purpose of arbitration and contradicts standard contract law principles regarding dispute resolution.
perturbation.law_explanation: The FAA favors enforcement of arbitration agreements. By rendering the arbitration advisory and giving one party unilateral control over interpreting key contract terms, the modified clause weakens the enforceability of the arbitration agreement and runs counter to the FAA's intent to promote binding arbitration as a means of dispute resolution.

perturbation.justification: The changed text makes arbitration advisory and gives one party final say on interpreting confidential information, which directly undermines the purpose of arbitration. The law explanation states the FAA favors enforcement of arbitration agreements. The changed text weakens the enforceability of the arbitration agreement and runs counter to the FAA's intent to promote binding arbitration as a means of dispute resolution. Snippet 1 is a 'Document not found' error. Snippet 2 contains 'None', but based on the Law Explanation alone, the altered contract contradicts the general principle of binding arbitration favored by the FAA, thus creating a contradiction.
